title: Spindle Cell Tumor Workup (Soft Tissue)
summary: A minimal, type-valid pathway: start → morphology gate → decision → result.

n1.title: Start
n1.description: Review clinical context (age, site, depth), imaging, and gross. Confirm this is a spindle cell predominant lesion on H&E.

n2.title: Morphology gate
n2.description: Confirm key pattern-level features before ordering broad panels.
morphologyFeatures: Growth pattern: fascicular / storiform / patternless / herringbone
Vascular pattern: staghorn / branching / delicate capillaries

options.label: Patternless + staghorn vessels (consider SFT)
options.rationale: Classic architectural clue for SFT spectrum.

n3.title: Decision: SFT pathway
n3.description: If SFT is suspected, confirm with lineage-defining markers and exclude mimics.
molecularIndications: NAB2-STAT6 fusion testing if STAT6 equivocal or for challenging cases

n4.title: Decision: Smooth muscle pathway
n4.description: Confirm smooth muscle differentiation and assess malignancy parameters.

n5.title: Decision: PNST pathway
n5.description: Confirm neural crest differentiation and consider site-specific entities.

n6.title: Result: Solitary fibrous tumor favored
n6.description: Integrate morphology + STAT6 nuclear positivity. Risk stratify by size, mitoses, necrosis, and age as applicable.
confirmatoryStudies: Consider fusion testing if STAT6 negative/equivocal
pearls: Patternless architecture + staghorn vasculature is a strong clue.
STAT6 is the key confirmatory marker.
pitfalls: CD34 is supportive but not specific.
Beware cellular SFT vs other hypercellular spindle tumors.

n7.title: Stop: broaden differential
n7.description: If lineage is unclear, stop and broaden workup (consider keratin-positive sarcomatoid carcinoma, melanoma, synovial sarcoma, MPNST, etc.). Use targeted panels and correlate with imaging/gross.

n8.title: Result: Smooth muscle tumor favored
n8.description: If SMA/Desmin/H-caldesmon support SMC differentiation, classify based on site and malignant criteria.
pearls: Fascicles + blunt-ended nuclei + eosinophilic cytoplasm support SMC.
pitfalls: Myofibroblastic lesions can be SMA+ but desmin/H-caldesmon-.

n9.title: Result: PNST favored
n9.description: If S100/SOX10 support neural differentiation, subclassify with clinicopathologic correlation and consider perineurial markers as appropriate.
pearls: SOX10 is often more specific for Schwannian differentiation than S100 alone.
pitfalls: Melanocytic lesions can mimic PNST and share SOX10 positivity.

title: Lytic Bone Lesion: Giant Cell–Rich Differential
summary: Minimal pathway for giant cell–rich lytic bone lesions: start → decision → result/stop.

b1.title: Start
b1.description: Confirm radiology: lytic vs sclerotic, epiphyseal vs metaphyseal, and patient age. Review H&E for giant cell–rich pattern.

b2.title: Decision: giant cell–rich patterns
b2.description: Use radiology + distribution of osteoclast-type giant cells and stromal cytology.

options.label: Epiphyseal lesion in skeletally mature, uniform giant cells

b3.title: Result: Giant cell tumor of bone favored
b3.description: Correlate with epiphyseal location; consider H3F3A (G34) mutation testing if needed.
pearls: Mononuclear stromal cells are the neoplastic population.
pitfalls: Beware giant cell–rich osteosarcoma if malignant osteoid present.

b4.title: Result: Aneurysmal bone cyst pattern
b4.description: Primary ABC can show USP6 rearrangement; secondary ABC occurs over other lesions.
confirmatoryStudies: USP6 rearrangement testing if primary ABC suspected
pearls: Always exclude underlying lesion in secondary ABC.
pitfalls: Telangiectatic osteosarcoma can mimic ABC radiologically and grossly.

b5.title: Stop: consider aggressive/malignant bone tumor
b5.description: If malignant osteoid or marked atypia is present, stop and evaluate for osteosarcoma subtype (including telangiectatic) with full radiologic correlation and sampling.
stopConditions: Marked nuclear pleomorphism in stromal cells
